Category: Healthcare & Life Sciences: Medical Services, Pharmacy, Biotechnology, Hospitals
Description: Risks of misinterpreting test results with AI assistance.
Prerequisites: A basic understanding of common laboratory tests (such as blood panels or urinalysis) and familiarity with general healthcare or life sciences concepts will help you get the most from this course.
Learning objectives: By the end of this course, you will be able to identify the key risks associated with using AI tools to interpret laboratory results and explain how errors or misinterpretations can arise in clinical and healthcare settings. You will also apply practical strategies to critically evaluate AI-generated insights, ensuring that patient safety and diagnostic accuracy remain the central priorities in your practice.

lessons.Description: Laboratory test results can be complex, and many healthcare professionals and patients are increasingly turning to AI tools to help interpret them. This lesson explores how AI can support the understanding of lab results, while also highlighting the significant risks that can arise when results are misread or taken out of context. We will look at real-world examples where AI-assisted interpretation has led to confusion, missed diagnoses, or inappropriate clinical decisions. By the end of this lesson, you will have a clearer understanding of where AI can genuinely add value and where human expertise remains essential. The goal is to help you use AI tools more safely and responsibly within a clinical or personal health setting.
lessons.content: Risks of misinterpreting test results with AI assistance.

slides.subtitle: How AI shapes result interpretation today
slides.slidecontent:
• AI reads lab data fast
• Flags abnormal values instantly
• Supports, not replaces, clinicians
• Errors still occur
• Human review stays essential

slides.subtitle: Supporting, not replacing, clinical judgement.
slides.slidecontent:
• AI tools flag abnormal values and highlight patterns in lab data
• They do not diagnose
• Clinical context, patient history, and professional judgement remain essential
• AI can miss nuances that an experienced clinician would catch
• Always the clinician's responsibility to interpret and act on results

slides.subtitle: Where AI Judgement Can Fall Short
slides.slidecontent:
• AI trained on broad populations may miss patterns specific to individual patient history
• Rare conditions often underrepresented in training data
• A flagged "normal" result can still be clinically significant depending on context
• AI lacks access to the full picture (symptoms, medications, lifestyle factors)
• Overreliance on AI confidence scores can discourage appropriate clinical questioning
• Small data entry errors can skew AI outputs significantly

slides.subtitle: Where AI Errors Enter Clinical Decisions
slides.slidecontent:
• AI misreads rare conditions
• Normal ranges vary by patient
• Alerts ignored or overtrusted
• Context missing from results
• Errors reach clinical decisions

slides.subtitle: Patient history shapes every result's meaning.
slides.slidecontent:
• Recent medication changes that skew electrolyte or enzyme readings
• Pregnancy status
• Chronic conditions like kidney disease or diabetes altering normal reference ranges
• A patient's baseline (their personal "normal" often differs from population averages)
• Acute stress or recent strenuous exercise temporarily shifting certain markers
• Ethnic and genetic variation affecting how some results should be interpreted

slides.subtitle: Recognising Where AI Interpretation Falls Short
slides.slidecontent:
• AI tools often miss the clinical context behind a result, such as a patient's symptoms or recent medication changes
• Reference ranges vary between labs
• A flagged "abnormal" value may be entirely expected in certain patient groups, yet AI may still raise an alert
• AI cannot account for pre-analytical errors like haemolysis or an incorrectly labelled sample
• Always verify AI output against the full patient history before acting on any interpretation

slides.subtitle: Recognising Limitations Before Acting on Data
slides.slidecontent:
• AI flags, humans decide
• Results need clinical context
• Errors happen, stay alert
• Cross-check unusual findings
• Trust training over algorithms

slides.subtitle: Essential reminders for safe AI use
slides.slidecontent:
• AI assists, never decides
• Always verify with clinicians
• Context changes every result
• Errors carry real risks
• Your judgement still matters
• Seek training before using

scripts.script: Welcome to the course on AI in Lab Results Interpretation. In this opening lesson, we are going to look at how artificial intelligence is already changing the way laboratory results are handled in clinical settings. One of the biggest advantages AI brings is speed. It can process large volumes of lab data almost instantly, and it is particularly good at spotting values that fall outside the normal range, flagging them straight away for attention. That said, it is really important to understand what AI is and is not doing here. It is there to support clinicians, not to replace their judgement. AI is a tool, and like any tool, it is not perfect. Errors can and do occur, which is exactly why human review remains absolutely essential. A trained clinician still needs to look at the results, consider the full clinical picture, and make the final call. So let us explore how this all works in practice.

So before we dive into the practical side of things, it's worth being really clear about what AI is actually doing when it comes to lab results. These tools are designed to help you spot things quickly, flagging values that fall outside normal ranges or highlighting patterns that might be easy to overlook. But, and this is important, they are not making diagnoses. AI doesn't know that your patient has a complex history, or that a slightly unusual result is completely expected given their circumstances. It can miss the kind of nuance that an experienced clinician picks up almost instinctively. So think of AI as a helpful assistant that draws your attention to something worth looking at, rather than an authority telling you what to do. The responsibility for interpreting results and deciding how to act on them always sits with you, the clinician. That professional judgement is something no algorithm can replace.

So whilst AI can be genuinely useful in interpreting lab results, it is important to understand where it can go wrong. Most AI systems are trained on data from large, general populations, which means they can miss patterns that are unique to a specific patient, particularly if that patient has an unusual history or a rare condition. There is also a context problem. A result that AI flags as normal might actually be quite significant when you factor in a patient's symptoms, their medications, or their lifestyle. AI simply does not have access to all of that information. Another concern is overreliance on confidence scores. Just because AI presents a result with high confidence does not mean clinical questioning should stop. And finally, small errors in data entry, something as minor as a misplaced decimal, can lead AI to produce a very different output. So the message here is clear: AI is a support tool, not a substitute for informed clinical judgement.

So let's talk about where things can actually go wrong in practice, because this is really important. AI systems are trained on large datasets, but they can struggle with rare or unusual conditions that simply don't appear very often in that training data. On top of that, what counts as a "normal" result genuinely varies from patient to patient, depending on age, sex, pregnancy status, and other factors. If the AI isn't accounting for that context, it can flag something as abnormal when it isn't, or miss something that actually is. There's also a human behaviour element here. Clinicians can become so used to automated alerts that they either start ignoring them, or they trust them a little too much without applying their own judgement. And crucially, a lab result on its own doesn't tell the whole story. Without the broader clinical picture, errors can quietly work their way into real decisions about patient care. That's what we need to be mindful of.

So here's something really important to understand about AI and lab results. AI tools are trained on population-level data, but every patient sitting in front of you is an individual with their own unique story. Think about how many things can shift what a "normal" result actually means for that specific person. Someone who's just started a new medication might show unusual electrolyte or enzyme levels. A patient who's pregnant has a completely different set of reference ranges. Someone living with kidney disease or diabetes has a different baseline altogether. And speaking of baselines, many patients simply run slightly high or low on certain markers, and that's perfectly normal for them. Even something as everyday as a hard gym session the day before can temporarily alter certain readings. Add to that the fact that ethnic and genetic background can influence how some results should be interpreted, and you can see why clinical context is absolutely essential. AI can process numbers, but it can't always see the full picture behind them.

Now, even the most sophisticated AI tools have real blind spots when it comes to interpreting lab results, and it is important that you know what those are. For a start, AI has no idea what is actually happening with your patient. It cannot see their symptoms, their medication history, or any recent changes to their treatment. It also does not always account for the fact that reference ranges differ from one laboratory to another, so a value flagged as abnormal in one setting might be perfectly acceptable in another. Certain patient groups, such as athletes or pregnant women, routinely produce results that look unusual on paper but are entirely expected. And AI simply cannot detect pre-analytical problems like a haemolysed sample or a mislabelled tube, which can completely distort a result. So the golden rule here is straightforward: never act on an AI interpretation in isolation. Always check it against the full clinical picture before making any decisions.

So, AI can be incredibly useful for flagging patterns in lab results, but it is really important that we do not simply accept what it tells us without thinking critically. The AI raises a flag, yes, but it is always a human clinician who makes the final decision. And that decision needs to be grounded in the full clinical picture, not just the numbers on a screen. Who is this patient? What are their symptoms? What is their history? AI does not know any of that unless we tell it. We also need to acknowledge that AI systems do make errors, so if a result looks unusual or does not quite fit, cross-check it. Look at it from another angle. And perhaps most importantly, your clinical training is your foundation. Algorithms are a tool to support your judgement, not replace it. Stay curious, stay alert, and always bring your own expertise to the table.

So let's bring everything together with some essential reminders before you start using AI tools in practice. First and most importantly, AI is there to support you, not to make decisions for you. Any output it produces must always be reviewed and confirmed by a qualified clinician. Remember that results never exist in isolation, because a patient's history, their medication, their symptoms, all of that shapes what a result actually means. And when AI gets it wrong, which it can, the consequences for a real patient can be serious, so we should never treat its suggestions as automatically correct. The good news is that your own professional judgement remains absolutely central to this process. AI does not replace your thinking, it informs it. Finally, please do make sure you have received proper training before using any AI tool in a clinical setting. Confidence built on solid foundations keeps both you and your patients safe. Keep these points in mind as we move forward.
